title: Variable Scope 🔭
description: Where do variables live and die?

slides.title: A Life and Death ☠️
slides.content:
Variables do not live forever.

Every variable is born, and every variable dies. 

The area where a variable is alive and usable is called its **Scope**.

slides.title: The Golden Rule of Scope 🧱
slides.content:
## Braces mean Boundaries

Variables belong to the block of code (the curly braces `{ }`) in which they are declared.

A variable defined inside a method **cannot be accessed** from outside that method. Once the method finishes, the variable is permanently destroyed.

slides.title: The Wall 🧱
slides.content: Try running this code and look at the error. The `main` method attempts to print `secretCode`, but that variable only exists inside the `hacker()` method.

quizData.question: If you declare `int count = 0;` inside an `if` statement block `{ }`, can you use `count` after the `if` block ends?

options.text: No, it is destroyed as soon as the `if` block's closing brace `}` is reached.
Only if using a while loop.

quizData.explanation: Variables are strictly scoped to the block where they are born. If declared inside an 'if' block, they die with the 'if' block.

slides.title: Why Scope Matters 🧠
slides.content:
This seems annoying, but it is actually a massive feature.

Because variables die, you can have a variable named `i` in one loop, and another variable named `i` in a different loop later on, and they won't conflict with each other! 

What happens in Vegas, stays in Vegas.
